title: Perspectives for dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cells
abstract:
Perspectives for Dye-sensitized
Nanocrystalline Solar Cells
Michael GraÈ tzel*
Laboratory for Photonics and Interfaces, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, CH-1015 Lausanne, Switzerland
The dye-sensitized solar cells (DYSC) provides a technically and economically
credible alternative concept to present day p±n junction photovoltaic devices. In
contrast to the conventional systems, where the semiconductor assumes both the task
of light absorption and charge carrier transport, the two functions are separated here.
Light is absorbed by a sensitizer which is anchored to the surface of a wide band gap
semiconductor. Charge separation takes place at the interface via photo-induced
electron injection from the dye into the conduction band of the solid. Carriers are
transported in the conduction band of the semiconductor to the charge collector. The
use of transition metal complexes having a broad absorption band in conjunction with
oxide ®lms of nanocrystalline morphology permits the harvesting of a large fraction
of sunlight. Near-quantitative conversion of incident photons into electric current is
achieved over a large spectral range extending over the whole visible region. Overall
solar (standard AM1.5) to electric conversion eciencies over 10% have been
reached. There are good prospects for producing these cells at a lower cost than
conventional devices. Here we present the current state of the ®eld. We shall discuss
new concepts of the dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cell (DYSC), including solid
heterojunction variants, and analyze the perspectives for future development of the
technology into the next millennium. Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
INTRODUCTION
P
hotovoltaic devices are based on the concept of charge separation at an interface of two materials
of dierent conduction mechanism, normally between solid-state materials, either n- and p-type
regions with electron and hole majority carriers in a single semiconductor material, hetero-
junctions between dierent semiconductors or semiconductor±metal (Schottky) junctions. In photo-
electrochemical cells, the junctions are semiconductor±electrolyte interfaces. In recent years, despite
prolonged eort, a disillusion has grown about the prospects of electrochemical photoeects at these
interfaces giving rise to competitive photovoltaic devices, since those semiconductors with band gaps
suciently narrow for ecient optical absorption of visible-light photons are necessarily insuciently
stable against photocorrosion. The width of the bandgap is a measure of the chemical bond strength. The
semiconductors stable under illumination, typically the ceramic oxides of reactive metals such as titanium,
therefore have a wide band gap, an absorption edge towards the ultraviolet and a consequent insensitivity
to the visible spectrum. A breakthrough represented by the separation of the optical absorption and the
charge separation processes in photo-electrochemistry, was realized by the association of a redox dye as
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 10 June 1999
Revised 30 September 1999
PROGRESS IN PHOTOVOLTAICS: RESEARCH AND APPLICATIONS
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
Millennium
Special Issue
* Correspondence to: Michael GraÈ tzel, Laboratory for Photonics and Interfaces, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, CH-1015
Lausanne, Switzerland.
 light-absorbing material with the wide band gap semiconductor.1 This patent-protected concept is now
well known in principle and has been licensed for industrial development.2±6 This attention is evidence of
the established credibility of the principle, and its progress towards practical applications.7±13 In the
following, the operational principles of the DYSC, the historical development of dye-sensitized photo-
voltaics and its current status will be brie¯y discussed. The main part of the paper will give perspectives on
the issues and opportunities associated with this technology for the next 20±50 years.
OPERATION PRINCIPLE OF THE DYE-SENSITIZED NANOCRYSTALLINE
SOLAR CELL (DYSC)
A schematic presentation of the operating principles of the DYSC is given in Figure 1. At the heart of the
system is a mesoporous oxide layer composed of nanometer-sized particles which have been sintered
together to allow electronic conduction to take place. The material of choice has been TiO2 (anatase),
although alternative wide band gap oxides such as ZnO and Nb2O5 have also given promising results.14,15
Attached to the surface of the nanocrystalline ®lm is a monolayer of the charge transfer dye. Photo-
excitation of the latter results in the injection of an electron into the conduction band of the oxide. The
original state of the dye is subsequently restored by electron donation from the electrolyte, usually an
organic solvent containing redox system, such as the iodide/triiodide couple. The regeneration of the
sensitizer by iodide intercepts the recapture of the conduction band electron by the oxidized dye. The
iodide is regenerated in turn by the reduction of triiodide at the counterelectrode, the circuit being
completed via electron migration through the external load. The voltage generated under illumination
corresponds to the dierence between the quasi-Fermi level of the electron in the solid and the redox
(Nernst) potential of the electrolyte. Overall, the device generates electric power from light without
suering any permanent chemical transformation. Accelerated endurance tests have shown that
ruthenium based charge transfer sensitizers can sustain 100 million turnovers without decomposition,
Figure 1. Principle of operation and energy level scheme of the dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cell (DYSC)
photoexcitation of the sensitizer (S) is followed by electron injection into the conduction band of the mesoporous
oxide semiconductor. The dye molecule is regenerated by the redox system, which itself is regenerated at the counter
electrode by electrons passed through the load. Potentials are referred to the standard calomel electrode (SCE)
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
172
M. GRAÈTZEL

of sunlight. Near-quantitative conversion of incident photons into electric current is

solar (standard AM1.5) to electric conversion eciencies over 10% have been
reached. There are good prospects for producing these cells at a lower cost than

technology into the next millennium. Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

regions with electron and hole majority carriers in a single semiconductor material, hetero-
junctions between dierent semiconductors or semiconductor±metal (Schottky) junctions. In photo-
electrochemical cells, the junctions are semiconductor±electrolyte interfaces. In recent years, despite

therefore have a wide band gap, an absorption edge towards the ultraviolet and a consequent insensitivity
to the visible spectrum. A breakthrough represented by the separation of the optical absorption and the
charge separation processes in photo-electrochemistry, was realized by the association of a redox dye as

* Correspondence to: Michael GraÈ tzel, Laboratory for Photonics and Interfaces, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, CH-1015
light-absorbing material with the wide band gap semiconductor.1 This patent-protected concept is now
well known in principle and has been licensed for industrial development.2±6 This attention is evidence of
the established credibility of the principle, and its progress towards practical applications.7±13 In the
following, the operational principles of the DYSC, the historical development of dye-sensitized photo-
voltaics and its current status will be brie¯y discussed. The main part of the paper will give perspectives on
the issues and opportunities associated with this technology for the next 20±50 years.
OPERATION PRINCIPLE OF THE DYE-SENSITIZED NANOCRYSTALLINE
A schematic presentation of the operating principles of the DYSC is given in Figure 1. At the heart of the
system is a mesoporous oxide layer composed of nanometer-sized particles which have been sintered
together to allow electronic conduction to take place. The material of choice has been TiO2 (anatase),
although alternative wide band gap oxides such as ZnO and Nb2O5 have also given promising results.14,15
Attached to the surface of the nanocrystalline ®lm is a monolayer of the charge transfer dye. Photo-
excitation of the latter results in the injection of an electron into the conduction band of the oxide. The
original state of the dye is subsequently restored by electron donation from the electrolyte, usually an
organic solvent containing redox system, such as the iodide/triiodide couple. The regeneration of the
sensitizer by iodide intercepts the recapture of the conduction band electron by the oxidized dye. The
iodide is regenerated in turn by the reduction of triiodide at the counterelectrode, the circuit being
completed via electron migration through the external load. The voltage generated under illumination
corresponds to the dierence between the quasi-Fermi level of the electron in the solid and the redox
(Nernst) potential of the electrolyte. Overall, the device generates electric power from light without
suering any permanent chemical transformation. Accelerated endurance tests have shown that
ruthenium based charge transfer sensitizers can sustain 100 million turnovers without decomposition,
Figure 1. Principle of operation and energy level scheme of the dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cell (DYSC)
photoexcitation of the sensitizer (S) is followed by electron injection into the conduction band of the mesoporous
oxide semiconductor. The dye molecule is regenerated by the redox system, which itself is regenerated at the counter
electrode by electrons passed through the load. Potentials are referred to the standard calomel electrode (SCE)
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
corresponding to 20 years of cell operation under natural light conditions. Dye-sensitized solar cells have
maintained their performance over 14,000 h of continuous light soaking.16
Figure 2 shows the scanning electron micrograph of a typical TiO2 (anatase) ®lm deposited by screen
printing on a conducting glass sheet (R  8±10 O/square) that serves as current collector. The ®lm
thickness is typically 5±20 m and TiO2 mass about 1±4 mg/cm2. Analysis of the layer morphology shows
the porosity to be about 50%, the average pore size being 15 nm. The prevailing structures of the anatase
nanoparticles are square-bipyramidal, pseudocubic and stablike. According to HRTEM measurements
the (101) face is mostly exposed followed by 100 and 001 surface orientations.
A recent alternative embodiment of the DYSC concept is the sensitized heterojunction,17 usually with
an inorganic wide band gap nanocrystalline semiconductor of n-type polarity as electron acceptor, the
charge neutrality on the dye being restored by a hole delivered by the complementary semiconductor,
inorganic or organic and of p-type polarity. The prior photoelectrochemical variant, being further
advanced in development, has an AM1.5 solar conversion eciency of over 10%, while that of the solid-
state device is, as yet, signi®cantly lower.
HISTORICAL BACKGROUND AND PRESENT DYSC PERFORMANCE
The history of the sensitization of semiconductors to light of wavelength longer than that corresponding
to the band gap has been presented elsewhere.18,19 It is an interesting convergence of photography and
Figure 2. Scanning electron microscope picture of a nanocrystalline TiO2 (anatase) ®lm used in the dye-sensitized
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
photoelectrochemistry, both of which rely on photo-induced charge separation at a liquid±solid interface.
The silver halides used in photography have band gaps of the order of 2.7±3.2 eV, and are therefore
insensitive to much of the visible spectrum, as is the TiO2 now used in these photoelectrochemical devices.
The ®rst panchromatic ®lm, able to render the image of a scene realistically into black and white, followed
on the work of Vogel in Berlin after 1873,20 in which he associated dyes with the halide semiconductor
grains. The ®rst sensitization of a photoelectrode followed shortly thereafter, using a similar chemistry.21
However, the clear recognition of the parallelism between the two procedures, a realization that the same
dyes in principle can function in both22 and a veri®cation that their operating mechanism is by injection of
electrons from photo-excited dye molecules into the conduction band of the n-type semiconductor
substrates23 date from the 1960s. In subsequent years, the idea developed that the dye could function most
eciently if chemisorbed on the surface of semiconductor.24±26 The concept emerged to use dispersed
particles to provide a sucient interface,27 then photoelectrodes with high surface roughness were
employed.28,29 Titanium dioxide became the semiconductor of choice. This material has many advantages
for sensitized photochemistry and photoelectrochemistry: it is a low cost, widely available, non-toxic and
biocompatible material, and as such is even used in health care products, as well as domestic applications
such as paint pigmentation. The standard dye at the time was tris(2,20-bipyridyl, 4,40-carboxylate)
ruthenium(II), the function of the carboxylate being the attachment by chemisorption of the chromo-
phore to the oxide substrate. Progress thereafter, until the announcement in 1991 of the sensitized
nanocrystalline photovoltaic device with a conversion eciency at that time of 7.1% under solar
illumination,1 was incremental, a synergy of structure, substrate roughness and morphology, dye photo-
physics and electrolyte redox chemistry. That evolution has continued progressively since then, with
certi®ed eciency now over 10% (see example in Figure 4 for I/V characteristics).
PERSPECTIVES FOR FUTURE RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT
The ideal sensitizer photovoltaic cell converting standard global AM1.5 sunlight to electricity should
absorb all light below a threshold wavelength of about 900 nm, which is equivalent to a semiconductor
with a band gap of 1.4 eV. For a single-junction device, this presents the optimal threshold energy where
the solar to electric power conversion eciency reaches its maximum of 33%.30 In addition, it must also
carry attachment groups, such as carboxylate or phosphonate, to ®rmly graft it to the semiconductor
oxide surface. Upon excitation, it should inject electrons into the solid with a quantum yield of unity. The
energy level of the excited state should be well matched to the lower bound of the conduction band of the
oxide to minimize energetic losses during the electron transfer reaction. Its redox potential should be
suciently high that it can be regenerated via electron donation from the redox electrolyte or the hole
conductor. Finally, it should be stable enough to sustain about 108 turnover cycles, corresponding to
about 20 years of exposure to natural light.
Much of the research in dye chemistry is devoted to the identi®cation and synthesis of a sensitizer
matching these requirements, while retaining stability in the photoelectrochemical environment. The
attachment group of the dye ensures that it spontaneously assembles as a molecular layer upon exposing
the oxide ®lm to a dye solution. This molecular dispersion ensures a high probability that, once a photon
is absorbed, the excited state of the dye molecule will relax by electron injection to the semiconductor
conduction band. However, the optical absorption of a single monolayer of dye is weak, a fact which
originally was cited as ruling out the possibility of high eciency sensitized devices, as it was assumed that
smooth substrate surfaces would be imperative in order to avoid the recombination loss mechanism
associated with rough or polycrystalline structures in solid-state photovoltaics. This objection was
invalidated by recognizing that by the injection process the electron ®nds itself in the semiconductor
lattice, separated spatially and by a dipole potential barrier from the dye. The surface dipole is generated
by proton transfer from the carboxylate groups of the sensitizer to the oxide charging the solid positively
and leaving an excess negative charge on the dye. The resulting electric ®eld promotes electron injection
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
into the solid while impairing the reverse process. Further retardation arises from the large driving force
of the back reaction which exceeds signi®cantly its reorganization energy (`inverted Markus region'31).
The energy released during the electron transfer must be digested by vibrational excitation of the ambient.
The higher the driving force, the less likely such a resonant phonon coupling will occur. Recapture of an
electron by the oxidized dye is therefore highly unlikely, given that the kinetics of its reduction by the
contacting electrolyte is much faster. Equally, capture of conduction band electrons by the redox
electrolyte is kinetically disfavored.32 For these reasons, the external quantum yield of photocurrent
generation in the DYSC is close to one if the optical losses in the conducting glass are accounted for. The
basis for the insensitivity of dye-sensitized electrochemical interfaces to parameters such as surface texture
is now obvious, and the use of nanocrystalline thin ®lm structures with a roughness factor of over 1000
The best photovoltaic performance, both in terms of conversion yield and long term stability, has so far
been achieved with polypyridyl complexes of ruthenium and osmium. Sensitizers having the general
structure ML2(X)2 where L stands for 2,20-bipyridyl-4,40-dicarboxylic acid, M is Ru or Os and X presents
a halide, cyanide, thiocyanate,33±35 or water substituent, are particularly promising. Thus, the ruthenium
complex cis-RuL2(NCS)2, known as N3 dye, has become the paradigm of heterogeneous charge transfer
sensitizers for mesoporous solar cells. The fully protonated N3 has absorption maxima at 518 and
380 nm, the extinction coecients being 1.3 and 1.33  104 Mÿ1 cmÿ1, respectively. The complex emits at
750 nm, the lifetime being 60 ns. The optical transition has MLCT (metal-to-ligand charge transfer)
character: excitation of the dye involves transfer of an electron from the metal to the p* orbital of the
surface anchoring carboxylated bipyridyl ligand, from where it is released within less than 100 femto-
second36,37 into the conduction band of TiO2 generating electric charges with unit quantum yield.
Discovered in 1993,34 the photovoltaic performance of N3 has been unmatched by virtually hundreds
of other complexes that have been synthesized and tested since then. Only recently has a credible chal-
lenger been identi®ed with the `black dye' (tri(cyanato)-2,20200-terpyridyl-4,40400-tricarboxylate)Ru(II).38
Figure 3 compares the spectral response of the photocurrent observed with the two sensitizers. The
incident photon to current conversion eciency (IPCE) of the DYSC is plotted as a function of excitation
wavelength. Both chromophores show high IPCE values in the visible range. However, the response of the
black dye extends 100 nm further into the IR than that of N3. The photocurrent onset is close to 920 nm,
i.e. near the optimal threshold for single junction converters. From there on the IPCE rises gradually,
until at 700 nm it reaches a plateau of ca 80%. If one accounts for re¯ection and absorption losses in the
conducting glass, the conversion of incident photons to electric currents is practically quantitative over
the whole visible domain. From the overlap integral of the curves in Figure 3 with the AM1.5 solar
radiation, one predicts the short circuit photocurrents of the N3 and black dye-sensitized cells to be
16 mA/cm2 and 20.5 mA/cm2, respectively, in agreement with experimental observations. The overall
eciency (Zglobal) of the photovoltaic cell is calculated from the integral photocurrent density (iph), the
open-circuit photovoltage (Voc), the ®ll factor of the cell (FF) and the intensity of the incident light
At this stage, the con®rmed eciency obtained with the black dye is 10.4%, as shown in Figure 4. Further
development will concentrate on the enhancement of the photoresponse in the near IR region. Judicious
molecular engineering of the black dye structure would increase the extinction coecient substantially in
the 700±900 nm region. The goal is to obtain a DYSC having optical features similar to GaAs. A nearly
vertical rise of the photocurrent close to the 920 nm absorption threshold would increase the short circuit
photocurrent from currently 20.5 to about 28 mA/cm2. With the Voc and FF values in Figure 3, this would
raise the overall eciency to 14.2%.
An advantage of the DYSC with respect to competing technologies is that its performance is remark-
ably insensitive to temperature change. Thus, raising the temperature from 20 to 608C has practically no
eect on the power conversion eciency.34 The loss in open circuit voltage due to the increase in the dark
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
current is compensated by the improved mobility of the hole carrier (triiodide) in the electrolyte), as well
as the decrease of the charge transfer resistance of the counter electrode, augmenting the photocurrent
and the ®ll factor of the cell. As these opposing eects cancel each other, the eect of temperature on cell
performance is small. In contrast, conventional silicon cells exhibit a signi®cant decline in the overall
conversion yield, amounting to ca 20% over the range between 20 and 608C. Since the latter temperature
is readily attained under full sunlight, this feature of the injection cell is particularly attractive for power
generation under natural conditions. Stability studies have shown the DYSC to sustain temperatures of
708C without loss of performance.39
Organic sensitizers, dye cocktails, quantum dot dyes
When considering organic dye structures, porphyrins and phthalocyanines merit particular attention, the
former because of the analogy with natural photosynthetic processes,40 the latter because of their
photochemical and phototherapeutic applications.41 Further interesting candidates are cyanines and
Figure 3. Spectral response curve of the photocurrent for the DYSC sensitized by N3 and the black dye. The incident
photon to current conversion eciency is plotted as a function of wavelength
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
merocyanines, which are widely employed as sensitizers in silver halide photography. While the former are
probably too unstable for use in the DYSC, the latter are an interesting prospect,42 especially for
applications in the transparent and solid state versions of the cell that are discussed below.
A successful strategy to obtain a broad optical absorption extending throughout the visible and near IR
region is to use a combination of two dyes which complement each other in their spectral features. Such
dye cocktails have already been applied to mesoporous TiO2 ®lms in the form of mixtures of porphyrines
and phthalocyanines.43 The result was encouraging in as much as the optical eects of the two sensitizers
were found to be additive. In particular, there was no negative interference between the co-adsorbed
chromophores, opening up the way for testing a multitude of other dye combinations.
Semiconductor quantum dots are another attractive option for panchromatic sensitizers. These are
II±VI and III±V type semiconductors particles whose size is small enough to produce quantum
con®nement eects. The absorption spectrum of such quantum dots can be adjusted by changing the
particle size. Thus, the band gap of materials such as InAs and PbS can be adapted to match the optimal
value of 1.4 eV.44,45 During the last decade, a wealth of information has been gathered on the physical
properties of these materials and research is being pursued very actively. It has been shown that quantum
dots can indeed act as ecient sensitizers for mesoporous oxide ®lms.46,47 One problem with this
approach is the photocorrosion of the quantum dots which will almost certainly happen if the junction
contact is a liquid redox electrolyte. However, they are expected to display high stability in the solid state
hetero-junction device. The advantage of these sensitizers over conventional dyes is their very high
Figure 4. Photocurrent±voltage characteristic of a nanocrystalline photoelectrochemical cell sensitized with the
panchromatic `black' dye. The results plotted were obtained at the NREL calibration laboratory. A light source
simulating AM1.5 global solar radiation was employed, the incident intensity being 1000 W/m2
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
extinction coecient, allowing thinner ®lms of the mesoporous oxide to be used. This would reduce the
dark current, increasing Voc and the overall eciency of the cell.
Dyes for power windows and invisible photovoltaic cells
A dream of many architects is to make buildings with windows that would produce electric power from
light. For transparency they should transmit a signi®cant fraction of visible light. This requirement can
not be reconciled with the optical characteristics of conventional p±n junction solar cells. As semi-
conductors are threshold absorbers harvesting practically all the photons with energy above their band
gap, they are opaque in the visible. By contrast, for the DYSC it is feasible to employ sensitizers absorbing
selectively the near IR and UV part of the solar spectrum, while visible light passes through the cell. Dyes
such as naphtocyanines ful®l the optical requirement for such applications. In particular, they have a very
strong absorption feature in the 700±920 nm region, where the solar photon ¯ux is very intense. A device
that would capture all the photons in this wavelength interval alone could produce a short circuit current
of 14 mA/cm2 and the contribution from the absorption of UV light by the TiO2 would raise this to
15 mA/cm2. Using the standard iodide-based redox electrolyte, solar conversion eciencies of 7±8%
appear feasible. This is sucient to make the system economically attractive as the cell is integrated in the
existing glass elements of the building. Conventional PV modules are mounted on a metal support,
contributing substantially to the overall price of photovoltaic installations. Integrating the cells into
building facades would reduce this cost signi®cantly. Thus, power windows based on the DYSC concept
have, in principle, the potential to be realized, opening up an enormous potential for future architectural
exploitation of photovoltaic cells.
Mesoporous oxide ®lm development
When the dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cell was ®rst presented, perhaps the most puzzling phenom-
enon was the highly ecient charge transport through the sponge-like TiO2 layer. The mesoporous
electrodes are very much dierent to compact analogs because
(i) the inherent conductivity of the ®lm is very low,
(ii) the small size of the nanocrystalline particles does not support a built-in electrical ®eld and
(iii) the electrolyte penetrates the porous ®lm all the way to the back-contact making the semiconductor/
electrolyte interface essentially three-dimensional.
Charge transport in mesoporous systems is under keen debate today47±51 and several interpretations
based on diusion,48 as well as the Montrol-Scher model for random displacement of charge carriers in
disordered solids,51 have been advanced. However, the `eective' electron diusion coecient is expected
to depend on a number of factors, such as trap ®lling and space charge compensation by ionic motion in
the electrolyte. Therefore, theoretical eorts will continue, as there is a need for further in-depth analysis
of this intriguing charge percolation process.
On the material science side, future research will be directed towards synthesizing structures with a
higher degree of order than the random fractal-like assembly of nanoparticles shown in Figure 2. A
desirable morphology of the ®lms would have the mesoporous channels aligned in parallel to each other
and vertically with respect to the TCO glass current collector. This would facilitate pore diusion, give
easier access to the ®lm surface and allow the junction to be formed under better control. One approach to
fabricate such oxide structures is based on surfactant templates similar to the mesoporous M-41 zeolith
synthesis pioneered by the Mobil group.52 Alternative methods employ self-assembly of nanoparticles53 or
electrochemically induced directional growth of a metal oxide ®lm on conducting substrates. Superior
photo-electrochemical performance of such oriented ®lms with regards to random particle networks is
expected and initial results in this area are promising.
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
Higher cell voltage through molecular engineering of the interface
The high contact area of the junction nanocrystalline solar cells renders the understanding and control of
interfacial eects essential for future improvement of cell performance. It is important to determine
the nature of the exposed surface planes of the oxide and the mode of interaction with the dye. For the
adsorption of the N3 dye on TiO2 this is now quite well understood. The prevalent orientation of the
anatase surface planes is (101) and the sensitizer if adsorbed through two of the four carboxylate groups,
at least one of them being anchored via a bidentate con®guration bridging two adjacent titanium sites.54
Molecular dynamic calculations employing a classical force-®eld have been carried out to predict the
equilibrium geometry of the adsorbed sensitizer-state.55 More sophisticated ®rst principle density
functional (DF) calculations have also been launched recently56 to model the surface interactions of TiO2
with simple adsorbates, as well as the surface reconstruction eects resulting from the adsorption. The
DF-approach is particularly promising and will provide an important tool for future theoretical
Synthetic eorts will focus on the molecular engineering of sensitizers and co-adsorbents that enhance
the charge separation at the oxide solution interface. The structural features of the dye should match the
requirements for current recti®cation: in analogy to the photo®eld eect in transistors, the gate for
unidirectional electron ¯ow from the electrolyte through the junction and into the oxide is opened by the
photoexcitation of the sensitizer. The reverse charge ¯ow, i.e. recapture of the electron by the electrolyte,
should be impaired by judicious design of the sensitizer. The latter should form a tightly packed insulating
monolayer reducing the dark current. Alternatively, suitable co-adsorbants deposited jointly with the
sensitizer may yield the desired blocking eect.57 The gain in open circuit voltage can be calculated from
where n is the ideality factor whose value is between 1 and 2 for the DYSC,58,68 io is the reverse saturation
current and R and F are the ideal gas and Faraday's constant, respectively. Thus, for each order of
magnitude decrease in the dark current, the gain in Voc would be 59±118 mV at room temperature. Work
in this direction is indispensable to raise the eciency of the DYSC signi®cantly over the 15% limit with
the currently employed redox electrolytes.
Research eorts will be actively pursued to develop new redox electrolytes for DYSC applications.
Present cells employ triiodide/iodide dissolved in organic solvents, such as methoxy-acetonitrile or
g-butyrolacton. While the I-/I3-couple is attractive from its excellent stability and reversibility, as well as its
low visible light absorption and high diusion constants, its redox potential is not well adapted to that of
the presently employed sensitizers. The voltage loss resulting from the mismatch is 0.4 eV and 0.2 V for the
N3 dye and black dye respectively. By avoiding this loss, the conversion eciency of the N3 dye based cell
would be raised presently from 10 to 17%. The challenge for the chemist is to identify new redox couples
that are well matched to that of the sensitizer, while maintaining all the advantages of the iodide/triiodide
system. New organic solvents will be synthesized and gelatinizing agents will be employed59 to reduce their
volatility. Room temperature molten salts based on imidazolium cations are also promising.60 These are
nonvolatile liquids which are stable up to 4008C and have an electrochemical window of more than 5 V.
However, their high viscosity limits applications to the low current regime, i.e. indoor power supplies.
Photovoltaic performance stability
Stability tests on sealed cells have progressed signi®cantly over the last few years. These tests are very
important, as the dye, the redox electrolyte or the sealing, may fail under long term illumination. The N3
dye sustained 108 redox cycles without noticeable loss of performance, corresponding to 20 years of
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
continuous operation in natural sunlight. Alleged instability of the N3 sensitizer61 can be traced back to
lack of adequate conditions for regeneration of the dye. Other reasons for cell failure have been
inadequate sealing and the use of unstable or contaminated organic electrolytes. Only very few electro-
lytes based on solvents, such as methoxy-acetonitrile and g-butyrolactone have proved to be adequate for
the DYSC, i.e. pass the standard stability tests applied for PV outdoor modules, including thermal stress
for 1000 h at temperatures up to 858C. The application of speci®c puri®cation procedures to remove
oxidizable impurities from these solvents was indispensable to obtain high thermal stability. Numerous
accelerated tests have been performed to scrutinize any DYSC photodegradation.12,39,60 A recent light
soaking experiment, performed over 14,000 h of continuous full intensity light exposure16 has con®rmed
that this system does not exhibit any inherent instability, in contrast to amorphous silicon which due to
the Stabler-Wronski eect undergoes photodegeneration.
Scale up, in series connected modules and applications
Prototypes of small devices powered with dye-sensitized nanocrystalline electrochemical photovoltaic cells
are now appearing: for example, a watch by Swatch Group Corp. of Switzerland featured in the technical
press,62 and industrial progress has been reported on upscaling and stability.11,16 Thus the INAP/Germany
consortium has recently produced a 120 cm2 Z-type interconnected module having an eciency of 7%.11
The advent of panchromatic sensitizers facilitates the fabrication of semitransparent devices for windows
and other architectural integration, since there is no colour bias imposed. In the case of the black dye
discussed above, the opacity of the window can be determined by the TiO2 ®lm thickness. Similarly, for
devices such as the recently-announced `smart window' of NREL, in which the DYSC is associated with an
electrochromic counterelectrode,63 the new panchromatic dye could be advantageous. Other embodiments
incorporating specialized counterelectrodes can be projected in the future, such as integrated charge
storage devices. These would follow from experience now being acquired in the fabrication of `rocking
chair' secondary cells based on the insertion of lithium into a mesoporous oxide electrode.64
An innovative approach employs a monolithic triple layer structure65 where the nanocrystalline anatase
®lm, a porous space and the carbon counterelectrode are directly superimposed. Figure 5 presents a cross-
section of such a device which can be produced entirely by screen printing. First, the mesoporous TiO2
photoelectrode is deposited on a pre-structured transparent conductive ®lm on glass. This is followed by
the deposition of another porous oxide layer, typically ZrO2, which acts as an insulator between the
photoelectrode and the counterelectrode. To complete the device, a conductive layer which contains the
carbon catalyst is deposited. At this stage the three layers are annealed to remove the organic binder and
increase their mechanical strength. Upon cooling, the sensitizer and electrolyte are inserted, followed by a
Z-type interconnection is a strategy to address the main obstacle to the upscaling of cells and modules
to dimensions suitable for the large-scale photovoltaic conversion of solar energy: the high sheet
resistance of the transparent conducting oxide used as a current collector. An alternative method is to
apply a collector grid to the conducting glass. The material of choice is silver. Unfortunately, Ag
undergoes rapid corrosion in the presence of iodide-containing redox electrolytes and it has appeared
Figure 5. Scheme of a three-layer monolithic dye solar cell
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
impossible so far to protect the grid structure adequately. However, signi®cant progress has been made
recently to overcome this problem by coating the Ag with a glass ceramic,66 and the ®rst 10  10 cm sized
photovoltaic glass tiles have been produced delivering over 1 A short circuit current in sunlight. This is
undoubtedly a very promising development which might well be the method of choice to realize low cell
production cost for the DYSC, the target price being below 1 US$/peakwatt.67
Dye-sensitized solid state heterojunctions
The all solid state sensitized heterojunction solar cell oers a promising development strategy. Here the
redox electrolyte is replaced by a solid p-type semiconductor interpenetrating the nanocrystalline TiO2
structure, which would permit the charge neutralization of dye molecules after electron injection by its
hole transport properties. A cross-sectional view of such a device is shown in Figure 6 and the principle of
operation illustrated in Figure 7. There has been some exploration of the concept of dye-sensitization of
heterojunctions, associated with nanostructuring of the interface, with encouraging initial results.17,69,70
However, even with modern organic semiconductors such as the p-type hole conducting medium, it must
be admitted that with the issues of materials selection, compatibility and fabrication unresolved, this
device concept remains at a rudimentary stage of research and development.
Since the sensitizing dye itself does not provide a conducting functionality, but is distributed at an
interface in the form of immobilized molecular species, it is evident that for charge transfer each molecule
must be in intimate contact with both conducting phases. This applies to the porous wide band gap
semiconductor substrate into which the photoexcited chemisorbed molecules inject electrons, as well as to
the hole conductor. It is not immediately evident that an interpenetrating network of two conducting
solids can so easily be established that an immobilized molecule at their interface can exchange charge
Figure 6. Cross section of the dye-sensitized heterojunction PV cell
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 8, 171±185 (2000)
carriers with both. However, our initial results are promising.17 The hole transport materials (HTM) are
deposited by spin coating from the liquid phase in order to achieve the necessary intimate contact, thereby
introducing a solution of the conducting compound into the previously-sensitized nanostructure. The
HTM currently used is the spirobi¯uorene, proprietary to Hoechst,71 whose structure is shown in Figure 8.
As the single junction DYSC technology acquires maturity, there will be a drive for application of more
advanced concepts in order to gain eciency in photovoltaic power production. Tandem cells are a
straightforward target and have already been realized at the laboratory scale. A more sophisticated, but
highly promising, concept72 is that of a three level cell. The latter strategy is particularly attractive for dye-
sensitized solar cells, as there is a wide choice for sensitizer/relay combinations that would satisfy the
conditions for realizing devices with very high conversion eciencies.

Photoelectrochemical production of high energy chemicals
Apart from electric power, the dye-sensitized cells can in principle be used to produce chemical fuels
directly from sunlight. There is no doubt that this will be an important direction for future research, as
such `photosynthetic' cells solve one of the principle shortcomings of conventional PV cells, i.e. the lack of
capacity for energy storage. The `Holy Grail' of all photoconversion processes is the splitting of water into
hydrogen and oxygen by sunlight and this will be one of the primary targets of future research.73 The
`brute force' approach to achieve this goal is to employ a solid state photovoltaic cell to generate electricity
that is subsequently passed into a commercial-type water electrolyzer. Although eciencies obtained are
high, these devices are very expensive. Hence, the price of hydrogen produced this way cannot compete
with conventional sources. Photocatalytic water cleavage systems based on aqueous dispersions of
semiconductor particles are much cheaper, but their eciency is generally below 1%.74 They have the
additional drawback that hydrogen and oxygen are produced simultaneously. Apart from the problem of
gas separation, this produces a slow-down of the photoreaction as the two gases accumulate and back-
react with each other. A way to avoid the latter problem is to separate the hydrogen and oxygen generating
reactions, as reported recently by Arakawa et al.75 We have developed a tandem device that achieves the
direct cleavage of water into hydrogen and oxygen by visible light in collaboration with two partner
groups from the Universities of Geneva and Bern.76 This is based on the in-series connection of two
photosystems. A thin ®lm of tungsten trioxide or iron oxide absorbs the blue part of the solar spectrum.
WO3Fe2O3  h ! eÿ    hWO3Fe2O3
The valence band holes (h) created by band gap excitation of the WO3 or Fe2O3 serve to oxidize water to
while the conduction band electrons are fed into the second photosystem. It consists of the dye-sensitized
nanocrystalline TiO2 ®lm. The latter is placed directly behind the WO3 ®lm capturing the green and red
part of the solar spectrum that is transmitted through the top electrode. The photovoltage generated by
the second photosystem enables the generation of hydrogen by the conduction band electrons.
The overall reaction corresponds to the splitting of water by visible light. At this stage, the overall solar to
chemical energy conversion eciencies of this system is between 4 and 5%.
The dye-sensitized nanocrystalline solar cell is at present the only serious competitor to solid state
junction devices for the conversion of solar energy into electricity. It is the prototype of a series of
optoelectronic and energy technology devices exploiting the speci®c characteristics of this innovative
structure for oxide and ceramic semiconductor ®lms. Recent developments in the area of sensitizers for
these devices have led to dyes which absorb across the visible spectrum leading to higher eciencies. The
recent development of an all solid state heterojunction dye solar cell holds great potential for further cost
reduction and simpli®cation of the manufacturing of dye solar cells.
